pattern: Collapse an infinite grid into a handful of relevant points (start, target, special-road endpoints) and run a shortest-path search over the small graph those points and roads form.
hints: Ignore the infinite grid -- the only points that can ever matter to an optimal route are the start, the target, and the two endpoints of every special road, because walking between any other two points is always available at its Manhattan cost.
Model those points as nodes of a small graph: connect every pair with an edge weighted by Manhattan distance, plus one extra directed edge per special road at its listed cost.
Run a shortest-path search from start to target over that graph (Dijkstra works since all weights are non-negative); a special road is only ever used when it beats walking the rest of the way.

edge_inputs.description: statement example 1, one chained pair of special roads beats walking
statement example 2, no special road helps, direct Manhattan walk wins
statement example 3, a single special road plus walking on both sides
no special roads at all, answer is pure Manhattan distance
start equals target already, cost is zero regardless of irrelevant special roads
a special road that is strictly worse than walking must be ignored
the optimum requires chaining two separate special roads back to back
a single special road runs straight from start to target and beats walking

stress.description: random start/target box with special-road endpoints confined inside it, up to the max road count
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 200))
    sx = rng.randint(1, 500)
    sy = rng.randint(1, 500)
    tx = sx + rng.randint(0, 500)
    ty = sy + rng.randint(0, 500)
    roads = []
    for _ in range(n):
        x1 = rng.randint(sx, tx)
        y1 = rng.randint(sy, ty)
        x2 = rng.randint(sx, tx)
        y2 = rng.randint(sy, ty)
        cost = rng.randint(1, 100000)
        roads.append([x1, y1, x2, y2, cost])
    return [[sx, sy], [tx, ty], roads]
stress.note: up to 200 special roads, giving up to ~402 graph nodes for the Dijkstra search
